behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) in contracts
behaviors.justification: The core failure is semantic: the LLM fails to extract certain terms, especially indirect ones (embedded parties, conditional obligations). A mock always returns what you configure it to return, hiding the real problem—that the LLM doesn't understand contract language well enough to find subtle or euphemistic terms. Only running the real LLM on real contracts catches failures like 'missed the obligation hidden in the severability clause' or 'didn't recognize Corp B as a party when mentioned only as the governing law jurisdiction.' Non-determinism matters too: the same contract run twice might extract different terms depending on LLM state. This is the definition of Agentic: correctness depends on what a real AI/ML model produces.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor using the real LLM on test contracts with ground-truth key terms. Verify extraction accuracy (target ≥95%) for parties, dates, obligations, and penalties. Run multiple times on the same contracts to detect non-determinism. Include edge cases: corporations mentioned only in preamble, obligations embedded in penalty clauses, penalty amounts hidden in definitions sections.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags problematic language, including indirect or euphemistic phrasing
behaviors.justification: The failure is semantic understanding: the LLM doesn't recognize that 'adjustment clauses' or 'flexibility in performance' are high-risk language under the configured policy. This is not a data-processing problem; it's about the LLM's ability to match semantic intent against policy rules. A mock can be hardcoded to flag certain terms, but real risk assessment requires genuine language comprehension. The real contract might use novel phrasings the model hasn't seen (e.g., 'mutual forbearance' as euphemism for waived penalties), and the test must verify the model can still flag it. This is Agentic because outcomes vary based on the real LLM's semantic reasoning, not just code logic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the risk assessor on contracts containing euphemistic risk language using the real LLM and the configured risk policy. Include test cases with indirect language: 'flexibility in delivery schedules' (risky if flexibility=liability waiver), 'mutual adjustment provisions' (risky if adjustment=price override), 'best efforts' variants (risky if undefined standard). Verify all problematic phrasings are flagged. Run multiple times to check for semantic inconsistency.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: The failure is regulatory reasoning: the LLM doesn't understand that a contract violates Section 4.2 of jurisdiction X's regulations, or that a buried clause creates non-compliance. This requires the LLM to reason over regulatory knowledge, not just pattern-match terms. A mock cannot simulate the reasoning needed to determine if 'choice of law' language undermines compliance, or if a limitation clause conflicts with mandatory disclosure requirements. Real contracts have subtle non-compliance (e.g., embedded indemnification that violates consumer protection statutes), and the model's understanding of regulations and implications varies. This is Agentic because correctness depends entirely on the real LLM's regulatory reasoning capability.
behaviors.test_description: Run the compliance checker on contracts with known compliance violations using the real LLM and the configured jurisdiction. Include violations that require regulatory reasoning: a limitation clause that conflicts with mandatory liability rules, an indemnification clause that violates consumer protection statutes, missing disclosures required by law. Verify the checker identifies at least the major compliance failures. Test multiple jurisdictions if applicable to verify regulatory knowledge is jurisdiction-specific.

behaviors.description: Merge step preserves both assessments with attribution when two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic logic (not LLM-dependent), but the failure we want to catch is the merge step losing or overwriting assessments when orchestrated with three real reviewer modules. The behavior describes receiving outputs from reviewers, not abstract data. A Unit test of merge(input1, input2, input3) would verify pure logic but wouldn't validate how the merge step actually receives and processes output from the three reviewer components in the real system. An Integration test—mocking the three reviewers with realistic output structures and testing merge in context—catches the failure at the right boundary: the merge step operating on actual reviewer outputs with proper data flow. Could a Unit test catch the core logic error (losing an assessment)? Yes, but Integration is the minimum to verify the behavior as stated: merge working with outputs from reviewers.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Mock the clause extractor, risk assessor, and compliance checker to produce conflicting assessments on the same clause (e.g., extractor={clause_id:'A', assessment:'standard'}, risk_assessor={clause_id:'A', assessment:'high_risk'}, compliance_checker={clause_id:'A', assessment:'compliant'}). Wire all three into the pipeline and run the merge step. Verify the final merged report contains all three assessments with correct attribution (e.g., 'extractor says standard, risk_assessor says high_risk, compliance_checker says compliant'). Verify no assessment is overwritten or lost. Test edge cases: all three assessments conflict, two agree and one disagrees.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline completes correctly and without losing findings: dispatching to all three reviewers in parallel, collecting results, and merging into unified report for a real contract
behaviors.justification: This is end-to-end behavior across multiple agentic steps (three reviewers running in parallel). While an Integration test with mocked reviewers could verify orchestration logic works, the stated behavior—'for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings'—requires testing with real reviewers because real-world failures include: (1) unexpected output format from LLM that breaks collection logic, (2) one reviewer timing out while others complete, (3) findings lost due to data structure mismatches not caught by mocks, (4) the pipeline failing on certain contract structures only the real LLM encounters. The phrase 'for a real contract' appears twice, emphasizing that this is about real end-to-end behavior. Mocked reviewers with synthetic outputs can hide failures that only manifest with actual LLM output variability and timing. This is Workflow: multiple agentic steps in sequence/parallel that must work together, and we need the real components to catch real failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: End-to-end test: Run a real contract document through all three reviewers (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker) in parallel using real LLMs. Collect all findings before merge. Run the merge step. Verify every finding from every reviewer appears in the final unified report with correct attribution and reviewer source. Run the same contract multiple times (5+ iterations) to detect non-deterministic findings loss—scenarios where one run loses findings but another doesn't. Include contracts of varying complexity (short standard contracts, long complex agreements with many clauses) to verify the pipeline handles scale.
